items.prompt_md: Why does `backtrack` append `path[:]` (a copy) to `result` instead of appending `path` itself?
options: Because `path` keeps mutating as the recursion continues, so storing the same list reference would let later pushes/pops change subsets already recorded
Because Python lists cannot be nested inside other lists
Because it makes the recursion run faster
items.answer: Because `path` keeps mutating as the recursion continues, so storing the same list reference would let later pushes/pops change subsets already recorded
items.explanation_md: `path` is one shared, mutable list reused across the whole tree. Appending it by reference would mean every recorded subset is really the *same* object, and later `append`/`pop` calls would silently rewrite subsets you already thought were done — `path[:]` freezes a snapshot instead.

items.prompt_md: You need to output every possible subset of a small list — order within a subset doesn't matter, but you must consider both including and excluding each element. Which approach fits?
options: Backtrack: at each index, choose to include the element, recurse, then un-choose (pop) and recurse again to skip it
Sort the list, then use two pointers converging from both ends
Use a sliding window that expands and shrinks over the array
Build a hash map from value to index and look up complements
items.answer: Backtrack: at each index, choose to include the element, recurse, then un-choose (pop) and recurse again to skip it
items.explanation_md: "Every subset" with an independent include/exclude decision per element is the fingerprint for backtracking's choose-recurse-un-choose template — there's no order or window to exploit, just an exhaustive walk of the choice tree.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of generating all subsets of an n-element list this way?
items.explanation_md: There are 2^n leaves in the choice tree (one per subset), and recording each one costs O(n) to copy `path` into `result`, giving O(n * 2^n) total time and space.
